EQUAL

Yxnxllx2005

Summary:

Some moments stay with you forever.

For Blaze the Cat and Silver the Hedgehog, seven days in the Sol Dimension would leave an imprint on their hearts that neither of them would ever forget.

→ SILVAZE
→ SEMI-AU
→ CHALLENGE

Notes:

Note: This story follows seven different days in Blaze and Silver's lives, but they do not take place within the same week. There will be time skips between chapters as the story moves through different moments of their journey together.

As with my other stories, each chapter focuses on a specific day and the memories, emotions, and experiences that made it unforgettable.

You can also find my other stories on Wattpad under the username SonicFicZone (available in Spanish).

(See the end of the work for more notes.)

Chapter 1: ✩N° 01: Beach Day ✩

Chapter Text

He couldn't remember exactly when he had started noticing it.

Maybe it was in the smallest gestures, or in the glances that seemed to linger a second longer than they should have. Perhaps it was in the way the princess's voice softened whenever a certain young hedgehog happened to be nearby.

Whatever the reason, no matter how much he tried to ignore it, Gardon could no longer deny the obvious.

The first time he met Silver the Hedgehog had been some time ago.

Tall, with unruly quills and a presence that seemed caught somewhere between youthful innocence and the experience of a life far more complicated than it should have been. Leaner than the famous blue hero, yet carrying a smile that blended confidence and kindness in a way that was almost disarming.

He had arrived unexpectedly, much like other visitors before him.

What surprised Gardon, however, was not the visit itself, but the fact that Princess Blaze had invited him to explore the kingdom on such short notice.

There had been no ceremony. No planning. No formal announcement.

One ordinary day, she had simply decided to bring him there.

Just because.

"He's a friend from Sonic's dimension, though from a different timeline," Blaze explained that afternoon, sounding perfectly at ease.

Gardon had raised an eyebrow, unable to fully make sense of those words.

Then the young hedgehog, still smiling politely, took the time to elaborate.

"I'm from the future," he said. "Two hundred years ahead, to be exact."

The guardian studied him carefully.

After years of serving the princess, he had learned to be wary of anything unusual—especially things that defied simple logic.

Yet the silver hedgehog didn't seem dangerous.

If anything, there was a sincerity in his eyes that would have been difficult to fake.

What Gardon didn't realize at the time was that this meeting would become the beginning of something far more significant than a simple visit.

In fact, the first warning sign had not come from Silver at all.

It had come from Marine.

The young raccoon had rushed over with her usual boundless energy and, unable to contain her excitement, blurted out in front of everyone:

"I've been wanting to meet you for ages! Blaze's always talking about how amazing and noble you are. You're like a prince straight out of a fairy tale!"

Under normal circumstances, there would have been nothing unusual about such a comment.

The unusual part was Blaze's reaction.

The Princess of the Sol Dimension—who could remain composed through almost any situation—suddenly froze. A faint blush spread across her face beneath her fur, and her gaze darted away for a moment before she quickly tried to recover.

"Marine is exaggerating a little," she said, attempting to regain her usual calm tone. "But it's true that after all the adventures we've shared, she knows quite a lot about you."

She said it quickly.

Too quickly.

As though she feared that any moment of silence might reveal more than she intended.

From where he stood, Gardon noticed the way Silver's eyes lit up with surprise and quiet delight.

The young hedgehog looked genuinely happy by what he had heard, even if he didn't say so aloud.

A moment later, they exchanged a brief glance, followed by a soft laugh shared between them like a secret only they understood.

That was when Gardon first had the distinct feeling that the silver hedgehog was far more than an ordinary guest.

.

.

.

Time passed, and with it, the silver hedgehog's visits to the kingdom became far less sporadic than one might have expected.

There was always a reason—or at least, that was the explanation.

A mission that required coordination. A training session. Matters involving Eggman Nega that needed to be discussed.

But Gardon knew Blaze far too well not to notice that, somehow, she always seemed to find a reason for those meetings.

Just like this time.

The Sol Kingdom was alive with color and light.

The Summer Festival was one of the most anticipated traditions of the year. Music filled the plazas, food stalls lined the streets, fireworks would illuminate the night sky, and children ran excitedly toward the shore.

It was the kind of celebration that brought everyone together beneath the same sense of joy.

To the surprise of many—and, at the same time, to no one's surprise at all—the hedgehog from the future was there.

Silver moved through the crowd with the ease of someone who was no longer considered an outsider.

Several yards away, Gardon watched him carefully.

He still remembered the suspicious looks Silver had received the first time the citizens witnessed his telekinetic abilities.

Now, however, things were different.

The young hedgehog seemed to have earned a place in the hearts of the kingdom's people.

Some greeted him warmly as he passed.

Others, particularly the children, constantly asked him for favors. A few wanted him to lift them into the air for a moment so they could pretend they were flying. Adults often requested help carrying heavy crates or placing decorations high in the trees.

Silver responded to every request with patience and an easy smile, as though the title of "hero" he carried in his own era weighed nothing on his shoulders.

Perhaps because, in this dimension, he could allow himself to be an ordinary teenager.

Even if only for a little while.

Eventually, the young hedgehog managed to free himself from the crowd after completing every favor that had been asked of him.

He made his way toward the guardian with his usual calm demeanor.

Trailing behind him were at least half a dozen children, all talking over one another.

"Mr. Silver, are you going to stay here forever this time?" one of them asked, tugging insistently on his hand.

"I want to learn how to use powers like yours!" another exclaimed, bouncing excitedly beside him.

"Can you make me fly through the sky? My mom already said yes!" a third child added with complete seriousness, earning laughter from everyone nearby.

Gardon watched the scene with a certain paternal annoyance.

Silver appeared amused by the attention, but the children's enthusiasm was quickly becoming overwhelming.

"That's enough!" Gardon boomed, crossing his arms. "Can't you see you're smothering the poor boy? Give him some room to breathe."

The children froze immediately, as though they had been caught in the middle of a prank.

Before they could scatter, however, Silver raised a hand and smiled reassuringly.

"It's okay, Gardon," he said kindly. "I don't mind."

Then he crouched slightly so he could meet the children's eyes.

"But I need to go find Blaze now. She's waiting for me at the harbor."

The mere mention of the princess's name sent a wave of excitement through the group.

As if they had just learned a valuable secret, the children immediately dashed off to share the news with anyone willing to listen.

"Silver's meeting the princess at the harbor!"

The hedgehog offered Gardon a polite farewell before continuing on his way, calm but purposeful.

The guardian simply watched him leave, arms still crossed as he let out a quiet sigh.

A knot formed in his stomach.

One he couldn't quite identify.

It wasn't dislike.

Silver had proven himself courteous, brave, and remarkably easy to trust.

No.

It was something else.

It's not that I dislike him, Gardon thought.

It's that Blaze...

The princess cannot afford distractions like this.

Not now.

.

.

.

The harbor was bathed in the golden glow of the setting sun.

The sky had begun to melt into warm shades of orange, pink, and lavender, blending seamlessly with the sea below. In the distance, preparations for the festival filled the air with faint sounds of excitement, but here, by the water, everything felt quieter.

More peaceful.

Silver approached at an easy pace.

Then he saw her.

For a brief moment, he stopped.

Blaze wasn't wearing her usual attire.

Instead, she wore a light purple dress that moved gently with the sea breeze, elegant in a way that seemed effortless. A delicate golden ornament rested in her loose hair, while a few rebellious strands danced freely in the wind.

Without exaggeration, she was the most beautiful thing he had seen in a very long time.

"What do you think?" she asked, sounding calm despite the faint blush dusting her cheeks. "I chose this outfit for the festival."

Silver didn't even need a second to think.

"You look amazing."

Blaze glanced toward the horizon, but the small curve of her smile told him everything he needed to know.

Together, they walked to the edge of the pier and sat down, letting their feet dangle above the water.

The sunlight reflected across the sea, surrounding them in a warm glow that felt strangely intimate.

Blaze was the first to break the silence.

"Tell me, Silver... how has your visit been so far?"

He immediately turned toward her, pleased by the question.

"Great," he answered without hesitation. "Like every time I come here. Honestly, it couldn't be better."

Something fluttered unexpectedly inside Blaze's chest.

Her expression, however, remained composed.

"I really like being here," Silver continued, his voice softer now. "Actually... it feels like a second home."

For a moment, Blaze simply looked at him.

She could hear the sincerity in every word.

Silver had always been remarkably honest about his feelings.

"I'm glad to hear that," she replied at last, her voice gentle. "This kingdom can be lonely sometimes... even when it's surrounded by people."

She paused briefly.

"But if it's a place you want to keep coming back to... then perhaps it isn't quite so lonely after all."

Silver blinked.

The quiet confession hidden within those words did not escape him.

"Is that how you feel sometimes?" he asked. "Lonely?"

Blaze hesitated.

Her gaze drifted toward the sea.

"Responsibility can be isolating," she admitted. "But I don't complain. It's my duty."

"Even so..."

Silver leaned forward slightly, his amber eyes steady.

"You shouldn't have to carry everything by yourself."

Blaze looked at him.

"If you ever need help, call me. I don't care how long it takes the message to reach me."

His smile softened.

"I'll come."

The princess opened her mouth to respond, only to find herself unexpectedly speechless.

The promise was simple.

Yet somehow, it meant more than she wanted to admit.

To hide her reaction, she picked up a small stone and tossed it toward the water.

It sank immediately.

"No talent for that, apparently."

Silver laughed softly.

Picking up another stone, he flicked it across the surface. It skipped several times before disappearing beneath the waves.

"It's not about talent."

He glanced at her.

"It's about patience."

Blaze raised an eyebrow.

"Are we still talking about skipping stones?"

"You can interpret that however you'd like."

A smile tugged at the corners of her lips.

For a moment, neither of them looked away.

There was something growing between them.

Something they both understood.

Something neither of them seemed ready to say aloud.

The breeze swept through Blaze's violet hair once more.

Without thinking, Silver lifted a hand, intending to brush a loose strand away from her face.

Halfway there, he stopped.

Uncertain.

Blaze turned her head slightly toward him.

Not pulling away.

Not objecting.

The gesture alone was enough.

They exchanged a smile filled with a familiarity that no longer needed words.

For a moment, the world seemed to fade away.

The festival.

The kingdom.

Their responsibilities.

Everything.

Leaving only the two of them sitting side by side as time itself appeared to pause.

Blaze was the first to break the spell.

A playful glimmer appeared in her eyes.

"I have an idea."

Silver raised an eyebrow.

"Should I be worried?"

"Possibly."

Before he could ask another question, Blaze reached for the hair tie around her wrist.

She slipped it off and held it between her fingers.

Her hair was already loose, so she had no need for it.

"Stay still."

Silver immediately became suspicious.

"What are you—"

"Stay still."

The future hedgehog wisely chose not to argue.

With surprising focus, Blaze began adjusting his silver quills.

Carefully.

Patiently.

As though each unruly strand presented its own unique challenge.

Silver sat perfectly still and allowed her to work.

Not because he understood what she was doing.

But because he trusted her completely.

A faint warmth crept into his cheeks.

It had very little to do with the sunset.

Eventually, Blaze leaned back to admire her work.

"There."

Silver blinked.

Curious, he leaned over the water to examine his reflection.

His quills had been gathered back in a loose ponytail.

The result somehow made him look more relaxed.

More suited for a summer festival.

Definitely different.

"I look..."

He tilted his head.

"Different."

Blaze crossed her arms, pretending to evaluate him objectively.

"You look better than I expected."

Silver burst out laughing.

"That was a compliment, right?"

"Yes."

She paused.

"But don't let it go to your head."

"There it is."

Silver placed a hand dramatically over his chest.

"I was worried for a second."

A smile threatened to betray Blaze's composure.

"So you only invited me here to use me as a hairstyle mannequin?"

"If that's what you'd like to call it."

Silver narrowed his eyes.

Then, with a flick of telekinesis, he sent a splash of water flying toward her.

Blaze gasped.

"Silver!"

"What?" he asked innocently. "You said there was a festival. We're supposed to have fun."

The grin on his face made it impossible to take him seriously.

Blaze immediately retaliated.

A second later, both of them were laughing as water splashed back and forth between them.

For a brief moment, they weren't a princess and a hero.

Just two teenagers enjoying each other's company.

"You look ridiculous like that," Blaze finally managed between laughs.

"Ridiculous?" Silver placed a hand over his chest again. "I look incredible, and you know it."

Blaze shook her head.

"It's... acceptable."

Silver leaned back against the pier, resting on his hands.

"Coming from you, Princess, that's basically the highest praise imaginable."

The smile that followed was small.

Warm.

Genuine.

Then, without saying a word, Blaze leaned sideways and rested her head against his shoulder.

Silver froze.

Only for a second.

Soon, his shoulders relaxed, and a quiet smile spread across his face.

Neither of them moved.

Together, they watched the seagulls drift across the sky while lanterns throughout the harbor slowly began to glow.

The festival was about to begin.

With her eyes half-closed, Blaze spoke softly.

"The ceremony will start soon."

A pause.

"And we probably won't get another chance to talk until it's over."

Silver looked out toward the sea.

"Then we'll just have to make the most of this moment."

Blaze nodded.

Allowing herself to remain there a little longer.

Resting against the shoulder that somehow always made her feel safe.

The duties of a princess would call for her soon enough.

But this small pause...

This quiet moment...

Had been worth it.

Because for now, it belonged only to them.

.

.

.

A few meters away, partially hidden behind stacked crates and barrels along the pier, Marine watched the scene unfold with a grin stretching from ear to ear.

"Look at them! Just look at them! Now that's a date!" she whispered excitedly, practically vibrating in place. "And judging by the looks of it, it was a successful one."

Beside her, Gardon stood with his arms crossed and a deep frown etched across his face.

Every instinct he possessed as the royal guardian told him to look away, as though what he was witnessing was far too personal to observe.

Yet he didn't.

He watched Blaze smile.

Not the smile she offered her subjects.

Not the polite smile she wore during official duties.

This one was different.

A genuine smile reserved for a certain hedgehog from the future.

For a moment, Gardon's heart tightened.

Then he took a slow breath, allowing the salty sea air to fill his lungs.

By now, there was no point in denying it.

The signs had been there all along.

In the lingering glances.

In the effortless understanding they shared.

In their honest conversations.

In the way they seemed to forget about the rest of the world whenever they were together.

It doesn't matter how unusual his origins are, or what era he comes from.

As long as she's happy...

Then I will be too.

Even so, the concern of a protective father still lingered within him.

A young hedgehog from another dimension.

Another timeline.

A future filled with uncertainties.

So many things could go wrong.

And yet, Gardon could no longer deny a simple truth.

He had never seen the princess look so alive.

Nor so genuinely happy.

Not as she did when she was with Silver.

For the first time, he allowed the idea to stop feeling uncomfortable.

Silently, he accepted that the young hedgehog had earned far more than the kingdom's trust.

He had earned the heart of its ruler as well.

As Gardon watched her resting peacefully beside him, he found himself thinking that perhaps, after all, they would find a way to overcome whatever obstacles stood in their path.

As long as they remained together.

And with a little luck...

Marine might just be right.

Chapter 2: ✩N° 02: Sol Gala✩

Notes:

I love writing this fanfic because Silvaze has been with me since I was around ten years old, I think. 💜 It's always been one of my favorite ships, and it makes me incredibly happy to bring Silver and Blaze to life through these stories.

They've been special to me for many years, so being able to write new moments for them and share them with other fans is something I truly enjoy.

Chapter Text

Crystal torches lit throughout the palace cast golden reflections that illuminated every corner of the Grand Hall. Tall windows, draped with crimson velvet curtains that fell in perfect folds, framed the splendor of the room. At its center, a magnificent chandelier poured its radiance across the mirrors lining the walls, bathing the hall in an almost ethereal glow.

Along the sides of the chamber, elegantly arranged tables displayed crystal goblets and trays overflowing with exotic fruits, while impeccably dressed servants moved with practiced precision to attend to the guests.

The air, scented with fine perfumes, mingled with the gentle melody of royal musicians tuning their instruments, heralding the beginning of an evening destined to be remembered in the kingdom's history.

That night, the Sol Kingdom celebrated another commemoration of the Treaty of Harmony, an agreement established many years ago that strengthened alliances with neighboring nations and ensured the prosperity of its trade routes.

Tradition dictated that the treaty be honored with a grand ball every fifteen years.

As a result, this particular evening carried special significance.

It was the first time Blaze had been entrusted with the honor of organizing and inaugurating the ceremony herself.

High within her chambers, the princess studied her reflection in the mirror one final time.

Her gown, crafted from light fabrics and embroidered with golden threads, seemed designed to embody the very spirit of the kingdom. Its delicate lilac color deepened beneath the light, while subtle golden accents shimmered with every movement.

It was more than royal attire.

It was a symbol of her role as sovereign and guardian of the emeralds that protected her realm.

"Your Highness."

Rosemary's voice came from the other side of the door, warm despite its formality.

"It is time. Everyone is awaiting your presence so that the gala may begin."

For a brief moment, Blaze remained silent, her gaze never leaving her reflection.

"Very well," she replied calmly. "Tell them I'm ready."

She took a slow breath, adjusted the tiara resting upon her brow, and allowed herself a final moment of composure.

The evening was not meant for her.

It was for her people.

Yet somewhere deep inside, a secret anticipation caused her heart to beat just a little faster.

When she descended the grand staircase, the murmur of conversation throughout the hall grew louder.

Representatives of the Sol Kingdom's nobility, foreign ambassadors, and trusted advisors had all gathered beneath the same roof.

Yet Blaze paid them little attention.

Among the countless elegant gowns and lavish suits, she searched for only one person.

And she found him.

Silver stood at the far end of the hall, visibly uncomfortable amidst so much formality. He was clearly unaccustomed to this sort of environment.

He wore a princely suit in a soft ivory shade, adorned with cyan details that accentuated the warmth of his amber eyes.

The outfit suited him surprisingly well.

Though Blaze found herself smiling faintly at the thought that Gardon had almost certainly been the one responsible for convincing him to wear it.

Even so, the hedgehog from the future was there.

He had kept his promise to accompany her.

And when their eyes finally met across the room, the tension of the moment seemed to fade away.

Silver offered her a shy smile.

Blaze could not help returning one equally genuine.

She came to a stop upon the dais, allowing the echo of her footsteps to fade throughout the immense hall.

Silence spread like a tide as every guest waited for her to speak.

Blaze drew a steady breath.

Then, with the composure that had always defined her, she began her address:

"Dear citizens of the Sol Kingdom, honored guests, ambassadors from neighboring lands, and all those gathered here tonight," Blaze began, her voice clear and steady, carrying a natural warmth throughout the hall. "Tonight, we celebrate a bond that extends beyond borders. The Treaty of Harmony we commemorate today is more than a political agreement; it is a symbol of the unity and trust we have built together over the years."

She paused briefly, allowing her gaze to sweep across the room.

"May this ball serve as a reflection of that alliance—a reminder that true strength is found in unity, cooperation, and mutual understanding. Let us continue to uphold these values for many centuries to come."

A graceful round of applause echoed throughout the hall, though most of the guests maintained the composed dignity expected of royalty and nobility.

Blaze inclined her head slightly in gratitude before continuing.

"Following our tradition, it is the princess's duty to open the first dance. And, as custom dictates, this waltz shall mark the beginning of tonight's celebration."

Curiosity immediately lit the eyes of many nobles.

Several had expected her to extend her hand to a powerful duke or a carefully chosen young aristocrat selected for the occasion.

In front of her, a line of young noblemen stood in orderly formation, each dressed in their finest attire.

They waited expectantly, their posture impeccable and their eyes fixed upon the princess as they wondered who would receive such an honor.

A murmur began to spread throughout the crowd.

Speculation.

Anticipation.

The inevitable guessing of a fortunate name.

But Blaze had already made her choice.

With measured steps, she descended from the dais and crossed the center of the ballroom.

Without the slightest hint of hesitation, her eyes searched for Silver among the crowd.

She found him exactly where she had seen him before.

Standing quietly at the edge of the room.

Somehow, even surrounded by nobles adorned in gold and jewels, he still managed to stand out.

A heavy silence settled over the hall as the princess extended her hand toward him.

The gesture was so unexpected that several nobles exchanged looks of disbelief.

Others openly disapproved.

For a moment, the silver hedgehog froze.

He wasn't sure whether he should accept.

The weight of countless eyes upon him was almost overwhelming.

Then Blaze smiled.

Softly.

And every trace of uncertainty vanished.

"Silver the Hedgehog..." she said, her tone perfectly natural, as though this were the most obvious choice in the world. "Would you honor me with this dance?"

Silver's heart nearly stopped.

With the slightest tremor in his hands, he stepped forward and gently took hers with utmost respect.

"Y-Yes..." he murmured, his voice barely audible beyond the space between them. "It would be my honor, Your Majesty."

At their conductor's signal, the musicians began the opening notes of a waltz.

The melody rose with effortless grace, filling the hall with its harmonious rhythm as Blaze and Silver stepped toward the center of the ballroom, directly beneath the lights that hung above them like a sky scattered with stars.

At first, Silver moved with a certain stiffness, far too aware of the attention focused upon them.

Ballroom dancing had never been one of his strengths.

He possessed only a basic understanding of the steps—enough to keep pace with the music, but lacking the polished elegance of those who had been raised among royal traditions and courtly dances.

His movements were correct.

Yet they lacked the effortless refinement the occasion seemed to demand.

Blaze, however, guided him with quiet confidence.

A slight pressure of her hand.

A subtle shift of her gaze.

That was all it took to direct him.

And little by little, trusting her completely, Silver began to relax.

The music carried him forward, and in surrendering himself to its rhythm, he discovered an unexpected confidence.

As though, for once, he could stop thinking and simply feel.

Then something happened that no one had anticipated.

Soft flames began to flicker around Blaze's gown.

Subtle at first.

Elegant.

Golden sparks danced through the air with every turn she made, rising like glowing embers around her.

Almost instinctively, Silver responded.

His telekinesis lifted the sparks gently into the air, guiding them into graceful spirals around the pair.

The floating embers soon became countless points of light, swirling around them like a constellation crafted just for the two of them.

"Don't stop," Blaze murmured with a knowing smile, her amber eyes shining as brightly as the flames surrounding them. "We've got this."

Silver's lips curved into a smile of his own.

"I wouldn't dream of it."

His gaze never left hers as he guided their next movement.

A collective murmur swept through the audience.

No one had expected the opening waltz to transform into a display of magic.

Yet the perfect synchronization between the princess and the hedgehog made it impossible to look away.

A court accustomed to formal, structured dances was now witnessing something that felt almost celestial.

Together, Blaze and Silver completed another graceful turn.

Then another.

And another.

As the music approached its finale, Silver lowered his forehead slightly toward hers.

At that exact moment, the flames gathered above them.

The embers converged into a radiant crown of fire that glowed like molten gold over their heads.

Blaze closed her eyes for a brief second, allowing herself to be enveloped by that shared display of trust and power.

Silver barely seemed to breathe.

Surrounded by hundreds of guests, he was aware of only one person.

Her.

The orchestra struck its final note.

At once, the crown dissolved.

The embers scattered into countless shimmering sparks, fading through the air like a shower of falling stars.

Silence filled the ballroom.

For one brief moment, not a single noble dared move.

Then the applause erupted.

Foreign ambassadors applauded enthusiastically, openly amazed by what they had witnessed.

The kingdom's advisors, though striving to maintain their composure, could not hide their astonishment.

Not everyone shared that admiration, however.

Among the older and more conservative nobles, uneasy whispers began to spread.

Some carried clear notes of disapproval.

The fact that the princess had chosen Silver—a young hedgehog with no aristocratic lineage—to open such an important celebration was already controversial enough.

That they had then turned the dance into such a remarkable spectacle only made it more scandalous in their eyes.

Blaze knew it.

She could feel the divided gazes upon them—some filled with admiration, others with criticism.

Yet she did not let go of Silver's hand.

Not even for a moment.

As for Silver, he was still struggling to process everything that had just happened.

His heart continued to race, and although the applause echoed throughout the ballroom, the only thing that truly mattered to him was the smile on Blaze's face.

As the applause gradually faded, the musicians shifted to a lighter melody, and the dance floor soon filled once more with couples eager to dance after the unforgettable spectacle they had just witnessed.

Blaze and Silver remained at the center of the ballroom for a moment longer, as though neither wished to break the bubble they had created around themselves.

After a few seconds, they resumed dancing, moving at a much gentler pace than before.

Blaze was the first to speak, lowering her voice so that only he could hear.

"You did very well."

Silver blinked, still looking unconvinced.

"Me?" he asked with a nervous smile. "I nearly stepped on your dress three times."

A soft laugh escaped her lips, barely louder than a whisper.

"No one noticed."

A small pause followed.

"And even if they had, I wouldn't have cared."

Silver looked at her again, touched by her sincerity.

Within those golden eyes, he saw not only the composure of a ruler, but also the genuine warmth of someone who, among everyone present, had chosen him.

"Blaze..." he murmured, a hint of hesitation slipping into his voice. "Why me? There was an entire line of nobles waiting for you to choose one of them."

For several seconds, she remained silent, as though carefully considering how much she wished to reveal.

Then, with the same honesty that had always defined her, she answered:

"Because they could have offered me a dance..."

Her gaze softened.

"But only you could have given me a moment I would remember forever."

The silver hedgehog found himself completely speechless.

Warmth rushed to his face, and for the first time that evening, he let out a genuine laugh.

"Then..."

His smile widened.

"Thank you for choosing me."

In that moment, he felt like the happiest person in the entire dimension.

Blaze inclined her head slightly, as though that simple gesture were enough to bring the conversation to a close.

But before she looked away, she added in a voice barely above a whisper:

"Don't thank me."

For an instant, her eyes met his.

"Just promise me you won't forget this dance."

Silver stared at her, feeling butterflies erupt in his stomach.

No one had ever asked something so personal.

So meaningful.

The light in Blaze's eyes seemed to draw him in completely.

And when he finally spoke, his voice trembled with emotion.

"I could never forget it."

Chapter 3: ✩N°03: Secret Date Night✩

Notes:

(See the end of the chapter for notes.)

Chapter Text

Night had fully settled by the time Silver followed Blaze through one of the oldest corridors on the outskirts of the palace.

Torches lined the walls at perfect intervals, their low flames casting wavering reflections across the polished stone floor. The light stretched their shadows behind them, long and silent companions in the darkness.

Neither of them spoke.

Blaze walked ahead with quiet confidence, her posture straight and regal. A few steps behind, Silver followed carefully, his gaze fixed on her back.

He knew she was risking far too much for him.

The flawless ruler, guardian of the Sol Emeralds, had begun to appear at his side far more often than the royal court would ever consider appropriate. They spent too much time together—more than anyone expected, more than anyone approved of. No one dared voice their suspicions aloud, but the looks they received were becoming increasingly difficult to ignore.

That was why they had started being more careful.

More discreet.

Even so, the thought weighed heavily on Silver. Every time he considered the effort Blaze made to balance her duties with the feelings she kept hidden from the world, a knot tightened in his chest.

Suddenly, Blaze came to a stop.

Before them stood an ordinary stone wall, indistinguishable from countless others throughout the palace. Ancient symbols had been carved into the columns on either side, their meanings long forgotten.

Without turning around, Blaze raised a hand and traced her fingers along one of the carvings until she reached a precise spot. She pressed down.

A faint click answered her touch.

The sound was so subtle it could easily have been mistaken for the natural groan of old stone.

The wall shifted.

Only slightly, but enough to reveal a narrow doorway hidden in plain sight.

Silver held his breath.

He had memorized the palace blueprints out of simple curiosity when he first arrived, yet this passage had never appeared on any of them.

Blaze gave him a small gesture, inviting him to follow.

Together, they stepped into the concealed corridor.

Their footsteps were muffled by a faded carpet that stretched across the stone floor. The ancient walls were covered with weathered carvings, barely visible in the dim light, as though they preserved stories long forgotten by history itself.

The spiral staircase ahead wound upward into darkness.

Each step creaked softly beneath their weight, a reminder of how long this place had remained untouched.

When they finally reached the top, a warm glow greeted them.

The staircase opened into a circular chamber far larger than Silver had expected.

Dark wooden bookshelves lined the walls from floor to ceiling, overflowing with leather-bound volumes, rolled parchments, and small gilded chests. The air carried the scent of aged paper, candle wax, and a faint trace of incense, as though time itself had chosen to rest within these walls.

Blaze crossed the room and gently ran her fingers along the spines of several books, as if greeting old friends.

Then she moved toward the chamber's lone window.

Beyond it, the city stretched into the night, illuminated by countless flickering lights.

Silver joined her.

The moment he looked outside, his breath caught in his throat.

“This place is beautiful...” Silver murmured, more to himself than to her.

The kingdom stretched out before them, dotted with countless lights pulsing in the distance. Ancient streets wound between elegant buildings, while gardens and fountains emerged here and there, shimmering softly beneath the moonlight. A river cut through the city, reflecting the torchlight in a pale ribbon that disappeared into the horizon.

From up there, everything seemed peaceful.

Perfectly balanced.

Almost unreal.

“It is,” Blaze replied after a brief silence, a rare softness touching her voice. “And that's exactly why almost no one comes here.”

Silver turned toward her.

Blaze stepped away from the window and rested a hand against the frame, leaning back slightly.

“This library belonged to the rulers who came before me,” she continued with a quiet sigh. “All of them. Including my parents. It wasn't a place for the royal court or scholars.”

Her gaze drifted across the room.

“It was more of an escape.”

She took a few slow steps backward before offering him a small smile.

“All kinds of secrets are kept here,” she added. “Decisions that weren't always the right ones. Doubts. Fears. Thoughts a ruler would never openly share.”

The weight of her words settled over him.

This wasn't simply a hidden room.

It was something far more personal.

An inheritance built from the voices of generations of monarchs—thoughts and confessions the people of the kingdom would never know existed.

“Blaze...” he said quietly, lowering his voice almost instinctively. “Are you sure it's okay for me to be here?”

She turned to face him.

There was no hesitation in her golden eyes.

Only certainty.

“Yes,” she answered. “Because I trust you.”

Silver fell silent.

The way she looked at him.

The warmth in her voice.

Those simple words carried more weight than any formal oath ever could.

He took a step toward her.

Just one.

Enough to leave only a small distance between them.

Then he leaned forward and pressed a gentle kiss to her forehead.

The gesture lasted only a moment—brief, restrained, yet filled with a quiet affection that words could never fully express.

When he pulled away, his voice dropped to little more than a whisper, as though the room itself might be listening.

“I won't let you down,” he promised, a faint smile touching his lips. “Never.”

Blaze froze for a second.

Heat immediately rushed to her cheeks.

She looked away and cleared her throat with as much dignity as she could manage.

“I...” she began, only to stop herself.

Then she tried again.

“I'm still not used to that kind of affection.”

Silver laughed softly, feeling embarrassed himself.

“Sorry,” he said quickly. “Marine keeps insisting it's normal for couples. She says it helps... I don't know. Express your feelings better.”

Blaze raised an eyebrow.

Though the amusement shining in her eyes completely undermined her attempt at seriousness.

“You really shouldn't take romantic advice from Marine.”

Her tone was resigned rather than scolding.

“Her definition of ‘normal’ tends to be highly questionable.”

A bright laugh escaped Silver before he could stop it, instantly dissolving the lingering tension from their secret journey through the hidden corridors.

“You're right,” he admitted, rubbing the back of his neck. “Marine is probably not the most reliable source when it comes to relationship advice.”

Blaze watched him in silence.

The torchlight from outside filtered through the window, wrapping his silhouette in warm shades of gold and violet. Despite her earlier comment, she didn't step away.

Quite the opposite.

She closed the distance Silver had left between them after the kiss on her forehead.

Her fingers found his and intertwined with them naturally, almost intimately. The gesture contrasted sharply with the composed dignity her title usually demanded of her.

“However...” she whispered.

The single word made Silver lower his head slightly to hear her better.

Blaze rose onto her tiptoes.

The movement was quick and deliberate, yet carried a vulnerability that completely disarmed him.

Her lips brushed the corner of his mouth.

Soft.

Barely more than a fleeting touch.

Yet the contact sent a jolt straight through him.

When she pulled away, the blush spreading across her cheeks revealed what words never could.

She was still learning this kind of love, too.

“Not everything Marine says is wrong,” she added with a shy smile before releasing his hand and turning toward one of the bookshelves.

Silver remained frozen for a moment.

His heart pounded so hard against his chest that he could hardly process what had just happened.

Then reality caught up with him, and he hurried after her.

“Come here,” Blaze said.

Her usual composure had returned, though there was a new brightness lingering in her eyes.

“I want to show you the books I used to read when I was younger. My favorites are all here.”

Silver leaned slightly over her shoulder, studying the worn spines lined across the shelves.

There were volumes on astronomy, travel chronicles, and ancient tales of knights and distant kingdoms.

“I'm sure they're all amazing,” he said honestly, admiring the elegant gold lettering on one of the covers. “You have good taste, Blaze. You always have.”

A faint smile touched her lips.

Carefully, she pulled out two books.

One was bound in deep blue velvet.

The other was decorated with intricate silver engravings that reflected the light like an old mirror.

Without hesitation, she held them out to him.

“You can keep these.”

Silver blinked.

“What?”

“Take them with you,” Blaze repeated as though it were the most natural thing in the world. “Read them when you're back in your own time. They'll help you understand this world better.”

A brief pause.

“And me.”

His eyes widened.

He accepted the books carefully, as though they might break beneath his fingers.

“Are you sure?” he asked softly. “These look irreplaceable. They're part of your family's history. They're too important to leave this place, especially with me. My world isn't exactly known for being stable.”

Blaze stepped closer.

Then she placed one hand over his.

Gently but firmly, she closed his fingers around the books.

The gesture carried trust above all else.

“I'm sure, Silver,” she replied, meeting his eyes without wavering.

“I don't worry about losing them.”

Her voice was calm.

Certain.

Because she believed every word she was saying.

“I know you'll bring them back to me.”

For a moment, neither of them spoke.

Then Blaze smiled.

A small smile.

The kind reserved only for him.

And the certainty in her voice felt like the greatest gift she had ever given him.

“After all,” she said quietly, “this is your home too.”

Her expression softened even further.

“And you'll always be welcome here.”

Notes:

💜 Thank you for reading. 💜

And I have to confess something...

Every single time the *"Legendary Lovers"* trend shows up on my TikTok feed, I secretly hope a Silvaze edit appears at the end. 😭💜 Because honestly? To me, they are the true *legendary lovers* of the Sonic universe.

So whenever I see that trend, my brain immediately goes:

*"Okay, but where are Silver and Blaze?"* jsjsjs.

Thank you for following this story and for all the support you've given it. ✨

Chapter 4: ✩N°04: Winter Day✩

Chapter Text

The Sol Kingdom looked truly beautiful that day.

During the night, snow had fallen, covering every corner of the kingdom with a delicate white blanket. Rooftops, stone pathways, and sprawling gardens had all disappeared beneath the shimmering layer, which sparkled beneath the first rays of dawn. In the distance, the mountains seemed to blend seamlessly into the pearl-gray sky, while a few lingering snowflakes continued to drift lazily over the city.

The air was cold—cold enough to make anyone want to stay indoors—but it also carried a strange warmth. A feeling difficult to describe, one that could easily be mistaken for the scent of firewood burning in chimneys, the soft chime of colorful decorations swaying in the breeze, or the distant laughter beginning to fill the streets.

Thus began a new day...

Merchants cleared away the snow piled in front of their shops before opening for business. Some families hung decorative wreaths on their front doors, while others finished stringing up glowing garlands that stood out against the pristine white landscape. Children raced from one side of the street to the other with their sleds, leaving tiny footprints in the freshly fallen snow, oblivious to the cold and completely immersed in the excitement of the season.

Because this was no ordinary morning.

There are traditions so ancient that they seem to defy every known rule of the universe.

It did not matter whether someone lived in a great city, a small village, another planet, another timeline, or an entirely different dimension. It did not matter what name the celebration received in each place or what unique customs accompanied it. Beyond all those differences, its essence remained unchanged: it was a time to gather together, share unforgettable moments, and express the affection we feel for those who hold an irreplaceable place in our hearts.

One way or another, this celebration would always find a way to endure...

Christmas.

Naturally, the Sol Kingdom was no exception.

Every year, when this season arrived, the streets filled with lights that seemed to compete with the stars themselves. Homes opened their doors to welcome family and friends, marketplaces overflowed with activity, and the entire kingdom became wrapped in an atmosphere of joy and togetherness.

Even those who normally preferred solitude found themselves, if only a little, swept up in the Christmas spirit.

And if anyone had been paying close enough attention, they might have noticed that not even the reserved and dignified princess of the Sol Kingdom was completely immune to it.

Blaze walked gracefully through the snow-covered streets of the city center. Her purple coat stood out against the blanket of white covering the roads, while a few snowflakes settled on her violet fur before melting away. At first glance, she appeared exactly as she always did: serene, refined, and unshakable.

Appearances, however, could be deceiving.

Because beneath that calm expression was an emotion she rarely allowed herself to acknowledge.

Her golden eyes wandered across the streets with a satisfaction she found difficult to conceal. She admired the decorations hanging between the buildings, the festive shop displays, and the families strolling together as they enjoyed the holiday season. It was a sight she had witnessed countless times throughout the years, and yet it never failed to feel special.

But this time, something was different.

Or rather, someone.

Silver was walking beside her.

The silver hedgehog gazed at everything around him with almost childlike wonder. His eyes jumped from one shop to another, from the Christmas wreaths hanging on doorways to the illuminated lanterns decorating the plaza, taking in every detail as though he were afraid of missing something important.

And the truth was, he was making no effort whatsoever to hide how much he was enjoying the experience.

Quite the opposite.

“I can’t believe how beautiful everything looks,” he commented, pausing for a moment to admire a massive glowing star suspended above the main avenue. “Seriously, Blaze... this is incredible.”

The princess turned slightly toward him.

“I’m glad you like it.”

The younger hedgehog let out a small laugh.

“Like it? I think that’s an understatement.”

His amber eyes shone with such genuine sincerity that Blaze had to look away for a moment, because it was impossible not to feel nervous when he looked at her like that.

It had been her who invited him to spend Christmas in her dimension.

And it had also been her who—though she would never admit it out loud—had spent days wondering how he would react upon discovering each of their Christmas traditions.

The idea had seemed pleasant from the very beginning.

Far too pleasant.

And that was something she preferred to keep to herself.

After all, Blaze was hardly the kind of girl who expressed her feelings easily.

Even so, there was one undeniable truth she could not ignore.

She was excited.

Perhaps because, out of all the places, times, and dimensions Silver could have chosen to visit, he had decided to spend those days there.

By her side.

The mere thought filled her chest with a pleasant warmth that contrasted beautifully with the winter chill.

Of course, she would never allow that feeling to show on the surface... or at least, she tried not to.

Because every time she heard the enthusiasm in the hedgehog’s voice, every time she saw him smile over something as simple as a cup of hot chocolate, she felt her heart beat faster than usual, as though it were insisting on reminding her of something she still was not ready to admit.

Meanwhile, Silver seemed completely incapable of containing his excitement, nor did he see any reason to try.

“Does it always look like this during Christmas?” he asked while examining a stall filled with handmade dolls.

“More or less.”

“More or less?”

“There are different decorations every year,” the feline explained with a faint smile. “My people really make an effort to ensure that each year feels more special than the last.”

“I see...” The amber-eyed hedgehog fell silent for a few seconds in thought. “Maybe that’s why everything here feels different.”

The princess raised an eyebrow.

“Different?”

“Yeah.”

The hedgehog from the future placed his hands behind his head as they continued walking.

“I’m not sure how to explain it. We celebrate Christmas in my era too, but... everything here feels calmer. More personal. Like the entire kingdom is connected somehow.”

Blaze remained silent for a few moments.

She had to admit it was not an inaccurate observation.

“I suppose the holidays have that effect. As long as I can remember, it’s always been that way...”

“And what about you?”

She turned toward him with slight hesitation.

“What about me?”

“What’s your favorite part of Christmas?”

The question caught her somewhat off guard.

She considered it for a moment before giving him an honest answer.

“I don’t know.”

“Oh, come on. There has to be something.”

“Silver...”

“The decorations?”

“No.”

“The food?”

“Not that either.”

“The presents?”

The princess let out a quiet sigh.

“Not everything revolves around presents. Don’t be so shallow.”

“I know, I know,” he replied quickly, raising his hands in surrender.

A barely noticeable smile appeared on the young princess’s lips.

“I think what I like most is seeing the kingdom like this.”

“Like what?”

She watched the families strolling through the plaza, the children playing in the snow, and the merchants chatting with one another.

“Happy.”

He looked at her for a few seconds.

And for some reason, that answer felt exactly like the kind of answer his best friend would give.

“Yeah...” he said softly at last. “That sounds a lot like you.”

Blaze frowned slightly, clearly intrigued by the comment. Before she could ask what exactly he meant, however, a familiar voice cut through the noise of the plaza.

“By the Sol Emeralds! What are you two lovebirds doing here?”

Both of them turned at the same time.

Running toward them was Marine, accompanied by a small group of children who could not have been older than eleven. They were bundled up in thick winter coats and dragged small wooden sleds behind them, leaving uneven trails in the snow.

The young raccoon wore the mischievous grin that always preceded a troublesome remark.

“Marine.”

The way Blaze pronounced her name was warning enough.

Naturally, the younger girl chose to ignore it.

“What?” she asked innocently.

“Don’t call us that.”

Marine burst out laughing.

“Relax, Your Highness. I’m only joking.”

Blaze narrowed her eyes. It was obvious she did not believe a single word.

As for Silver, he remained completely silent.

He did not protest.

He did not even bother denying the accusation.

He simply observed the exchange with suspiciously calm composure, as though he saw no need to intervene.

And that, of course, did not escape Marine’s notice.

The young raccoon shifted her gaze between the two of them for several seconds, instantly understanding what was happening between them, even if they had not realized it themselves—or simply refused to acknowledge it.

SHE WAS A VISIONARY.

A CRAZY WOMAN’S INSTINCT IS NEVER WRONG, AND SOONER OR LATER TIME WOULD PROVE HER RIGHT.

She had to bite the inside of her cheek to stop herself from laughing.

Because if Silver was not denying anything... well, that already said quite a lot on its own.

“Anyway, putting aside the couple of the hour...”

“Marine!”

“Alright, alright!” she replied, raising her hands. “Sorry. I won’t do it again.”

The young raccoon cleared her throat before pointing her thumb toward the group of children accompanying her.

“We’re on our way to the forest.”

“The forest?” Silver asked, immediately intrigued.

One of the children nodded enthusiastically.

“We’re going to cut down pine trees!”

“Every year we pick one to decorate our homes,” another girl explained. “It’s a tradition.”

The silver hedgehog’s eyes lit up instantly.

“Really?”

“Uh-huh.”

“You choose them yourselves?”

“Of course!”

“And then you decorate them?”

The children nodded in unison.

Silver looked more fascinated by the second.

“Wow... I’ve never heard of anything like that before.”

Marine caught that curiosity immediately, and a mischievous smile slowly spread across her face.

“Well... you could come with us if you want.”

“Really?” The boy genuinely looked eager to join them.

“Sure.”

Then the raccoon turned toward Blaze.

“What do you say, Princess? It could be fun.”

And judging by the way she smiled while saying it, it was obvious she was already imagining exactly how she could use the opportunity to tease her.

Blaze was about to refuse.

She truly was.

She could feel Marine’s expectant gaze on her, waiting for any excuse to mock her for the rest of the week. Besides, spending an entire morning in a snowy forest cutting down trees had not exactly been part of her plans for the day.

She opened her mouth to politely decline the invitation.

Then she looked at her best friend.

A grave mistake.

The younger hedgehog was staring at her with an expression that could only be described as a mixture of excitement, curiosity, and hope. His amber eyes looked almost puppy-like, as though this simple proposal had suddenly become the most exciting event of the morning.

“We could try it,” he said carefully. “Only if you want to, of course.”

Blaze felt part of her resolve beginning to crumble.

“Silver...”

“I’ve never done anything like this before...”

The way he said it was the final blow.

Marine had to turn her head away to hide her victorious grin.

“Fine,” the princess finally relented, crossing her arms. “But only for a little while.”

Silver’s expression—and everyone else’s—brightened immediately.

And, to Blaze’s surprise, that made the decision seem far less terrible.

“Great! You’ll see, you won’t regret it!”

“I’m not making any promises.”

“I’m trying to stay positive.”

“You’re failing.”

“I know.”

Marine watched the exchange with almost offensive satisfaction.

Oh, yes.

This was going to be absolutely hilarious.

.

.

.

The forest lay just beyond the outskirts of the kingdom.

As they moved farther away from the city, the sounds of the bustling streets gradually faded, replaced by the soft crunch of snow beneath their feet and the whisper of the wind weaving through the trees.

It looked like something straight out of a fairy tale.

After several minutes of walking, the group finally stopped in an area where numerous young pine trees grew.

“Alright,” one of the children announced with an air of authority. “Now comes the hard part.”

The hedgehog from the future glanced around.

“Choosing the tree?”

“No.”

“Finding the prettiest one?” Blaze added, unable to imagine what could be so important.

“Nope.”

“Then what?”

The child rummaged through one of the sleds and pulled out a strange metallic device.

At first glance, it vaguely resembled an axe, though it was clearly a far more modern version. The blade had been replaced by a glowing semicircular arc of condensed energy, while the handle featured several safety and stabilization systems.

“This is a Pine-Cutter LX-7.”

Silver blinked.

The thing immediately reminded him of the bizarre inventions Tails was always building in his workshop.

“A what?”

“A Pine-Cutter LX-7.”

“That sounds... sophisticated.”

“It is.”

The child nodded proudly.

“My dad helped design it.”

Blaze examined the device curiously.

“Years ago, people used regular axes,” another child explained, “but they were dangerous for younger kids. These generate an energy vibration that cuts through wood without causing accidents. If someone gets too close, they automatically shut down.”

Silver took the device in his hands.

“Wow...”

“Pretty cool, right?”

“Very.”

The hedgehog looked back at the trees before an idea crossed his mind.

“Although... technically, I could just pull one out with telekinesis. If you want, I could help and—”

The children looked horrified.

“That’s cheating!”

“Total cheating!”

“The worst kind of cheating!”

Silver visibly flinched.

He had not expected to be scolded by a group of children.

Blaze found the scene unexpectedly adorable. Truthfully, she found it rather amusing as well.

“Is it really that serious?” he asked as a feeling of guilt began settling into his chest.

“If you want to participate in the tradition, you have to do it properly. It’s the most important Christmas rule.”

Silver looked at Blaze.

Blaze looked at Silver.

Then they both shrugged almost simultaneously.

“I guess they have a point...”

The children scattered to search for their own trees while Blaze and Silver remained together, surveying the forest.

Eventually, they found a medium-sized pine whose shape was remarkably balanced.

“What do you think of this one?” the taller hedgehog asked, pointing toward the tree.

The princess tilted her head slightly.

“It’s nice.”

“That sounded unconvincing.”

“I’m being objective.”

“That sounded even less convincing.”

Blaze rolled her eyes at the irony.

“Fine. I like it.”

“Much better.”

Silver handed her the Pine-Cutter and flashed her a grin.

“Ladies first.”

The feline raised an eyebrow.

“And what if I decline such a privilege?”

“Then I’ll insist.”

“You’re stubborn.”

“Thank you.”

“That wasn’t a compliment.”

“I’ll take it as one.”

A small huff escaped Blaze’s lips.

Silver immediately smiled.

That alone was enough to make her look away because of the strange flutter she felt in her chest whenever they made eye contact.

Lately, it had been happening far too often.

She took the device and approached the tree.

“Alright. How difficult can this be?”

Five seconds later, she discovered it was considerably harder than it looked.

The vibration required steady pressure and a perfectly consistent motion.

“Need some help?” Silver asked, his voice carrying a hint of teasing.

“No.”

“You look like you need help.”

“I don’t.”

“You definitely do.”

Blaze clenched her teeth.

He was testing her patience.

Patience she did not possess.

“Silver...”

“I’m just trying to be helpful.”

Before she could protest again, he stepped behind her to adjust the position of the device.

“Look. Like this.”

His hand gently closed over hers.

The gesture was entirely innocent.

And yet...

Blaze felt her shoulders tense for the briefest moment.

Because suddenly, Silver was far too close.

She could feel the warmth of his body despite the cold forest air.

She could hear his breathing.

And she could catch that familiar, pleasant scent that always seemed to linger around him.

“See?” he asked, completely oblivious. “You just have to move it a little slower.”

“I... I understood that.”

“Then why wasn’t it working?”

“Because I was testing something.”

“What?”

“Your ability to come up with solutions.”

The excuse was pathetic, but she was far too proud to simply thank him.

Silver laughed.

And somehow, feeling the vibration of that laughter so close to her was surprisingly difficult to ignore.

Eventually, the two of them ended up holding the device together.

Their hands brushing now and then.

Their shoulders bumping accidentally whenever one of them adjusted their position.

Sharing small jokes while the trunk slowly began to give way.

It was a simple task.

A ridiculous one, really.

Very ridiculous.

Yet for some reason, neither of them seemed eager to finish it.

And as the children's laughter echoed through the snow-covered trees, Blaze allowed herself to think that perhaps this was becoming one of the best Christmases she had experienced in a very long time.

.

.

.

Several hours later, as the sun began to disappear behind the mountains and the sky was painted in soft shades of orange and violet, the palace's grand hall was bustling with activity.

At the center of the room stood the pine tree Blaze and Silver had chosen that morning. Its branches were gradually being covered with lights, garlands, and crystal ornaments that reflected the warm glow of the chandeliers. Several boxes lay open across the floor while Silver rummaged through them with obvious enthusiasm.

“What about this one?” he asked, holding up a golden ornament.

“More to your left.”

“Here?”

“A little more to the right.”

“And here?”

Blaze let out a resigned sigh.

“Silver, you're putting all the ornaments on the same side of the tree.”

“I’m creating an artistic composition.”

“You’re making it lopsided.”

“That’s also a form of art, Princess.”

The young feline gently shook her head, unable to suppress a small smile.

From the entrance to the hall, Gardon and Rosemary watched the scene in silence. Neither of them wished to interrupt. They simply observed as the two young royals debated the proper placement of ornaments while continuing to decorate together.

Rosemary was the first to speak.

“I’ve never seen the princess this happy.”

Gardon remained silent for a moment before nodding.

“Neither have I.”

His expression softened ever so slightly.

“It’s nice to see her like this,” the koala admitted with a hint of resignation.

Rosemary smiled.

“Even if it’s thanks to that young fellow?”

“Even if it’s because of him.”

The maid let out a quiet laugh.

At that moment, Silver glanced up and spotted them.

Blaze followed his gaze.

“Gardon? Rosemary?”

They had been discovered instantly.

Rosemary cleared her throat, looking slightly embarrassed.

“My apologies, Your Highness. We didn’t mean to interrupt.”

“Yes, you did,” Silver replied with a grin.

“Well, you both seemed to be having fun. We simply wanted to make sure everything was in order,” Gardon explained.

The younger hedgehog grabbed a box full of ornaments and waved a hand.

“Well, since you’re already here, you can help us.”

“Silver...”

“What?”

“You can’t give orders to the palace staff.”

“I’m not giving orders.”

The young hedgehog smiled, open and sincere.

“I’m inviting them to be part of this great moment,” he clarified immediately. “The more people who join in, the better it turns out.”

Rosemary exchanged an amused glance with Gardon.

And in the end, both of them stepped forward.

What had begun as a simple invitation soon turned into an afternoon filled with laughter, stories, and ornaments placed in locations of highly questionable appropriateness. Outside, the snow continued to fall over the Sol Kingdom, while inside the palace the atmosphere grew warmer with every passing hour.

None of them could have known it then, but it would be the first of many times.

Because the following year, they decorated another tree together.

And the year after that as well.

What had begun as a spontaneous Christmas activity eventually became a tradition.

After all, the best traditions are often born from simple moments shared with the right people.

And sometimes, that is how love is built, too.

Notes:

Hello everyone!

Thank you so much for making it this far and for reading this new Silvaze fanfic. This is a story I'm genuinely excited to share, and it makes me very happy to enjoy these moments between Silver and Blaze together with all of you.

I hope you enjoyed this chapter as much as I enjoyed writing it.

If you'd like to continue this journey with me and don't want to miss what comes next, feel free to save the story and stay tuned for future chapters!

I'd also like to mention that English is not my first language, but I'm doing my best because I wanted to contribute a little more content for this wonderful ship that means so much to me. 💜

If you notice any mistakes or have suggestions that could help me improve, please feel free to leave a comment. I'd truly appreciate your feedback and support.

Thank you again for reading and for all the encouragement.

See you in the next chapter!
